serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one bowl (approximately 350g) of pork ribs and old cucumber soup, including broth, pieces of old cucumber, and 2–3 pork rib segments.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Ribs and Old Cucumber Soup — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pork Ribs and Old Cucumber Soup (cr_id F06100031). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson, Foster-Powell, Brand-Miller
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pork Ribs and Old Cucumber Soup. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate Chinese broth-based soups with minimal starchy ingredients. Carbohydrate content is 1.65g/100g; glycemic load per 350g serving = (35 × 1.65 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 2.0, confirming negligible glycaemic impact. Estimation basis: Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed GI tables for comparable soup categories.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 260mg/100g; a 350g serving delivers approximately 910mg sodium, representing ~40% of the 2300mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit soup broth consumption and avoid adding salt during cooking, as a standard 350g bowl provides approximately 910mg sodium; consuming only the solids and discarding excess broth is recommended.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (1.65g/100g) and minimal sugar (0.83g/100g) result in negligible glycaemic load per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (1.65g/100g) and estimated GI of 35; however, patients should monitor total meal composition when the soup is served alongside rice or other high-GI staples, and limit the quantity of red dates used in preparation to minimise added natural sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork ribs contribute saturated fat and the sodium level (260mg/100g) is elevated relative to cardiovascular dietary targets.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this soup in moderation due to its saturated fat contribution from pork ribs and borderline sodium content; trimming visible fat from ribs before cooking and limiting broth intake can reduce both saturated fat and sodium load per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork ribs are a source of arachidonic acid (omega-6), which can promote pro-inflammatory cytokine production.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit frequency of consumption of this dish as pork ribs contribute arachidonic acid that may amplify inflammatory pathways; substituting pork ribs with fish or chicken would reduce the pro-inflammatory omega-6 load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Ribs and Old Cucumber Soup (排骨老黄瓜汤, Pái Gǔ Lǎo Huáng Guā Tāng) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese slow-simmered clear soup made by boiling pork ribs with old (mature yellow) cucumber, red dates (hong zao), and sometimes wolfberries or dried squid for several hours. The old cucumber — a fully ripened, yellow-skinned variety of cucumber — imparts a mild, slightly sweet flavour and a soft, silky texture to the broth. The soup is characteristically light, clear, and subtly sweet-savoury, with tender pork rib meat that falls off the bone after prolonged simmering. It is a staple of Singaporean home cooking and is commonly served as part of a multi-dish Chinese family meal alongside steamed rice. Nutritionally, the soup is low in calories (44 kcal/100g), low in carbohydrates (1.65g/100g), and provides moderate protein (5.07g/100g) primarily from the pork ribs. Sodium is elevated at 260mg/100g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic impact is negligible due to the very low carbohydrate content. Old cucumber is believed in traditional Chinese medicine to have cooling properties and is often consumed during hot weather. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free but is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets due to the pork content.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06100031) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from peer-reviewed analogous data due to absence of a direct GI measurement for this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this traditional Singaporean soup preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat estimated at 0.68g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central pork rib composition data (approximately 35% of total fat as saturated fat for braised/boiled pork ribs). GI of 35 is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed GI tables; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. The dish is not halal due to pork content. Keto dietary tag from input is retained as the carbohydrate content (1.65g/100g) is consistent with ketogenic dietary thresholds.
